source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That Compute Instances Do Not Have Public IP Addresses

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That Compute Instances Do Not Have Public IP Addresses
input_row.description: Compute instances should not be configured to have external IP addresses.
input_row.rationale: To reduce your attack surface, Compute instances should not have public IP addresses. Instead, instances should be configured behind load balancers, to minimize the instance's exposure to the internet. Impact: Removing the external IP address from your Compute instance may cause some applications to stop working.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. For every VM, ensure that there is no External IP configured. From Google Cloud CLI gcloud compute instances list --format=json 1. The output should not contain an accessConfigs section under networkInterfaces. Note that the natIP value is present only for instances that are running or for instances that are stopped but have a static IP address. For instances that are stopped and are configured to have an ephemeral public IP address, the natIP field will not be present. Example output: networkInterfaces: - accessConfigs: - kind: compute#accessConfig name: External NAT networkTier: STANDARD type: ONE_TO_ONE_NAT  Exception: Instances created by GKE should be excluded because some of them have external IP addresses and cannot be changed by editing the instance settings. Instances created by GKE should be excluded. These instances have names that start with "gke-" and are labeled "goog-gke-node".
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to go the the Instance detail page. 3. Click Edit. 4. For each Network interface, ensure that External IP is set to None. 5. Click Done and then click Save. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Describe the instance properties: gcloud compute instances describe <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE> 2. Identify the access config name that contains the external IP address. This access config appears in the following format: networkInterfaces: - accessConfigs: - kind: compute#accessConfig name: External NAT natIP: 130.211.181.55 type: ONE_TO_ONE_NAT 3. Delete the access config. gcloud compute instances delete-access-config <INSTANCE_NAME> --zone=<ZONE> - -access-config-name <ACCESS_CONFIG_NAME> In the above example, the ACCESS_CONFIG_NAME is External NAT. The name of your access config might be different. Prevention: You can configure the Define allowed external IPs for VM instances Organization Policy to prevent VMs from being configured with public IP addresses. Learn more at: https://console.cloud.google.com/orgpolicies/compute- vmExternalIpAccess Default Value: By default, Compute instances have a public IP address. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/backend- service#backends_and_external_ip_addresses 2. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- advanced#sshbetweeninstances 3. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting-to-instance 4. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/ip-addresses/reserve-static-external-ip- address#unassign_ip 5. https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy- constraints Additional Information: You can connect to Linux VMs that do not have public IP addresses by using Identity- Aware Proxy for TCP forwarding. Learn more at https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- advanced#sshbetweeninstances For Windows VMs, see https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- to-instance.
input_row.impact: Removing the external IP address from your Compute instance may cause some applications to stop working.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/load-balancing/docs/backend- service#backends_and_external_ip_addresses 2. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- advanced#sshbetweeninstances 3. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting-to-instance 4. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/ip-addresses/reserve-static-external-ip- address#unassign_ip 5. https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy- constraints Additional Information: You can connect to Linux VMs that do not have public IP addresses by using Identity- Aware Proxy for TCP forwarding. Learn more at https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- advanced#sshbetweeninstances For Windows VMs, see https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/connecting- to-instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control involves checking whether Google Compute Engine instances have public IP addresses, which can be easily verified through GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The audit instructions explicitly mention using the Google Cloud CLI to list instances and examine their network interfaces for external IP configurations. This property is part of the standard GCP service architecture and is accessible programmatically, meeting the criteria for an automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_instance_no_public_ip

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` to enumerate all instances within the project.
- For each instance, examine the `networkInterfaces` field to check if `accessConfigs` exists, which would indicate an external IP is configured.
- Specifically, ensure the absence of the `natIP` key which signals that no public IP is associated with the instance when it is stopped, and the `type: ONE_TO_ONE_NAT` in the network settings.
- Exclude instances created by Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) using the prefix "gke-" and label "goog-gke-node", as these are expected to have external IPs by design.
- Pass Criteria: An instance should have no external (public) IP address configured in its `networkInterfaces`, except for GKE nodes.
- Fail Criteria: Presence of `natIP` under `accessConfigs` for any non-GKE instance indicates non-compliance.

This automated check will validate each instance's network configuration, ensuring they are not publicly exposed, thus reducing potential external attack vectors per the CIS benchmark specifications.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. This control can and should be checked programmatically.
- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Capability:**  
  The GCP Compute Engine API, as well as the `gcloud` CLI, allow full enumeration of instance network interfaces and their public IP assignments. The command `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` is valid and provides the necessary data.
- **Other Automation Options:**  
  In addition to the CLI, this check could also be implemented using the REST API (`instances.aggregatedList`), or via GCP Config Validator (Forseti/Config Validator policies), but the CLI approach is appropriate and standard.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**
  - The use of `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` is correct for enumerating instances.
  - Checking the `networkInterfaces[].accessConfigs[].natIP` field is the right way to detect public IPs.
  - Exclusion of GKE nodes by name prefix (`gke-`) and label (`goog-gke-node`) is reasonable, though not fully robust (see Improvements).
  - The explanation of pass/fail criteria is clear and aligns with CIS intent.
- **Program Name Convention:**
  - The name `gcp_compute_instance_no_public_ip` is descriptive and follows the convention, but could be more explicit (see Improvements).
- **Minor Issues:**
  - The check for GKE nodes could be more robust (see below).
  - The statement about `natIP` not being present when the instance is stopped is misleading; the field may still exist, but the IP may be ephemeral or released. The check should focus on the presence of `natIP` regardless of instance state.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **GKE Node Exclusion:**
  - Relying solely on the `gke-` prefix and `goog-gke-node` label may miss some GKE nodes (e.g., custom-named node pools, or future changes in GKE labeling). Consider also checking for the presence of the `container.googleapis.com/cluster-name` label, which is more robust.
  - Document the rationale for GKE exclusion, as CIS sometimes expects all instances to be checked unless explicitly excluded by policy.
- **Alternative API Methods:**
  - For large orgs or multiple projects, consider using `gcloud compute instances aggregated-list --format=json` to enumerate across all zones.
  - The REST API (`compute.instances.aggregatedList`) could be used for more scalable automation.
- **Program Name:**
  - Suggest: `gcp_compute_instance_no_external_ip` (using "external" is more consistent with GCP terminology than "public").
  - For even more clarity: `gcp_compute_instance_networkinterface_no_external_ip`
- **Edge Cases:**
  - The check should clarify how it handles stopped instances, preemptible VMs, and VMs with ephemeral vs static external IPs.
  - Consider documenting how to handle service accounts or special-purpose VMs (e.g., bastion hosts) if exceptions are allowed by policy.
- **Output/Reporting:**
  - Recommend that the script output the instance name, zone, and offending external IP for easier remediation.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The approach is technically sound and aligns with GCP best practices and CIS requirements.
- Minor improvements are possible, but the core logic is correct.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **MODIFY**

- **Keep the automated approach.**
- **Modify** the GKE node exclusion logic to use more robust detection (e.g., check for `container.googleapis.com/cluster-name` label).
- **Clarify** the handling of stopped instances and ephemeral IPs in documentation.
- **Consider** renaming the program to `gcp_compute_instance_no_external_ip` for clarity and consistency.
- **Enhance** output to aid remediation.

---

**Summary:**  
The assessment is fundamentally correct and appropriate for automation. Some minor technical and naming improvements are recommended to align with best practices and ensure robustness, especially regarding GKE node detection and terminology.

---
